query: are cardenolides inhibitors of Na+/K+ ATPase?

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Potassium Pump -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As the Na + -K + ATPase is essential for maintaining various cellular functions, its inhibition could result in diverse pathologic states. Studies show that patients with heart failure have a 40% lower concentration of total Na,K-ATPase. [21] One significant clinical application is in cardiovascular pharmacology. For example, ouabain is a cardiac glycoside that inhibits the Na + -K + ATPase by binding to the K + site. Other cardiac glycosides, such as digoxin and digitoxin, directly inhibit the Na + -K + ATPase. [22] This inhibition causes a buildup of excessive K + extracellularly and accumulation of excessive Na + intracellularly as the Na + -K + ATPase can no longer pump K + into the cell or pump Na + out of the cell. This buildup of intracellular Na + hinders the concentration gradient that usually drives the Na + /Ca 2+ channel exchanger, which generally pumps Na + into the cell and Ca 2+ out of the cell because the concentration gradient is not favorable for Na + to enter the cell as excessive Na + has built up intracellularly. This indirect inhibition of Na + /Ca 2+ exchange causes a buildup of Ca 2+ intracellularly because the exchanger cannot allow Ca 2+ to exit the cell since it cannot accept Na + into the cell. This increases intracellular Ca 2+ and then increases cardiac contractility. This positive inotropy stimulates the vagus nerve, causing a decrease in heart rate. This physiology is clinically significant in treating heart failure as it increases the contractility of the heart. It is also clinically significant in treating atrial fibrillation as it decreases the conduction of the atrioventricular node and causes depression of the sinoatrial node. [23] Diuretic therapy has also been shown to reduce myocardial Na,K-ATPase when there is potassium loss. In contrast, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors could stimulate the activity of the Na, K pump. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Ibutilide -- Mechanism of Action -- Phase 4: Resting Phase
retrieved_docs.content: The remaining calcium ions get pumped out of the myocytes by secondary active transport through the Na+/Ca++ exchanger. Cardiac myocyte Na+/K+ ATPase is inhibited pharmacologically by the cardiac glycosides (digoxin). Inhibition of the Na+/K+ ATPase causes an increase in intracellular Na+ ions and leads to a series of biochemical changes, beginning with the reverse action of membrane-bound Na+/Ca++ exchangers. The change in polarity of Na+/Ca++ exchangers causes an efflux of Na+ and an influx of Ca++ to restore the resting membrane potential without Na+/K+ ATPase activity. The increased concentration of intracellular calcium is responsible for the positive inotropic properties of digoxin therapy. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Glycoside and Digoxin Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The primary mechanism of action of digitalis is the inhibition of the sodium-potassium ATPase pump within the myocyte. This reversible inhibition of the ATPase results in increased intracellular sodium levels. The build-up of intracellular sodium leads to a shift of sodium extracellularly through another channel in exchange for calcium ions. This influx of intracellular calcium results in increased myocyte contractility.

retrieved_docs.title: Digoxin Immune Fab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: DIF is an antidote used primarily for severe, life-threatening toxicity. Understanding digoxin's mechanism of action and toxicity profile is essential for comprehending its reversal effect. Digoxin, a potent cardiac glycoside, is used to treat heart failure and atrial fibrillation. This medication binds and inhibits the potassium ion subunit of the sodium/potassium pump (Na + /K + ATPase) in cardiac myocytes. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Digoxin Immune Fab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical studies have also highlighted the clinical utility of digoxin immune Fab in treating pre-eclampsia, an exaggerated inflammatory condition compounded by altered endothelial function. Pre-eclampsia involves widespread activation of platelets and WBCs and subsequent elevated levels of IL-γ, IL-6, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α). Digoxin immune Fab can also downregulate TNF-α-induced endothelial surface adhesion molecules (ICAM, VCAM, and E-selectin). DIF also reduces TNF-α-mediated downregulation of Na + /K + pumps. Maternal serum in women with pre-eclampsia also contains elevated endogenous digoxin-like factors (EDLFs), including cardenolide, ouabain, bufadienolide, and marinobufagenin. These factors can inhibit Na + /K + ATPase activity in erythrocytes and the mesenteric artery. Maternal hypertension during pre-eclampsia occurs when reduced Na + /K + ATPase activity contributes to the accumulation of intracellular calcium, leading to systemic vasoconstriction. Therefore, digoxin immune Fab can improve fetoplacental circulation and symptoms of eclampsia/pre-eclampsia by restoring cellular Na + /K + ATPase activity. Digoxin may also protect against TNF-α-mediated endothelial cell dysfunction to offset the exaggerated inflammatory state. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Digoxin Immune Fab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The pump maintains a relatively low sodium concentration inside the myocyte; inhibition results in an increased intracellular sodium concentration. Elevated sodium inhibits ion exchange by other transporters in the myocyte cell membrane. For example, the sodium-calcium exchanger (NCX) maintains low intracellular calcium levels in exchange for transporting sodium intracellularly. Digoxin-induced elevation of intracellular sodium decreases this exchange, resulting in elevated calcium levels within the myocyte. The available calcium is actively reabsorbed and stored by the sarcoplasmic endoplasmic reticulum ATPase (SERCA). The resulting increase in overall calcium available for cross-bridge formation between actin and myosin filaments ultimately leads to increased cardiac contractility (positive inotropic effect).

retrieved_docs.title: Digoxin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Digoxin exerts its effects through 2 primary mechanisms of action, selectively utilized based on the clinical indication: Positive ionotropic effect: Digoxin increases the force of cardiac contraction by reversibly inhibiting the myocardial sodium-potassium ATP (Na+/K+ ATPase) pump. This enzyme regulates ion movement in the heart. Digoxin increases intracellular sodium levels, which drives an influx of calcium into the heart, enhancing contractility, increasing cardiac output, and subsequently decreasing ventricular filling pressures. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sodium Potassium Pump -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There are reports of abnormal expression levels or activity of the Na + K + pump in diabetes, hypertension, Alzheimer disease, and in various tumors, including glioblastoma, non-small cell lung carcinoma, breast cancer, melanoma, colorectal carcinoma, and bladder cancer. [26] . Na + K + -ATPase and its endogenous regulators, the endogenous cardiac steroids, play a role in the etiology of bipolar disorder and are a potential target for drug development for the treatment. [27] RNA and DNA viruses can directly affect Na,K-ATPase function, particularly viral infections targeting the host cell components. Na,K-ATPase holds promise as an antiviral strategy to minimize the resistance to antiviral drugs and is effective. [28] Cardiac glycosides inhibit cytomegalovirus replication with an additive effect when combined with antiviral drugs such as ganciclovir. [29] Cardiac glycosides can also be active on other DNA viruses, such as herpes simplex virus, by inhibiting the expression of a viral gene. [30] There is evidence of a Na/K-ATPase oxidant amplification loop in the process of aging, obesity, and cardiovascular disease. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Transport -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A highly illustrative example of the importance of active transport is the use of cardiac glycosides like digoxin, which inhibit sodium-potassium ATPase in cardiac cells. Employing primary active transport, this protein normally acts to extrude sodium out of myocytes in exchange for potassium into the cells. In the presence of cardiac glycoside, the intracellular sodium will be higher. This indirectly inhibits the sodium-calcium exchanger, which normally brings sodium into the cell in exchange for calcium leaving. As such, more calcium is unable to leave the cell, so more calcium can act intracellularly to stimulate cardiac contractility or positive inotropy, implicating its usage in diseases that have decreased inotropy like heart failure. Because potassium is kept in the extracellular space, it can build up and cause hyperkalemia. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Aqueous Humor Circulation -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Active transport at the site of the non-pigmented epithelial cells is thought to be the major contributor to aqueous humor formation. The energy required for active transport is generated via the hydrolysis of ATP to ADP, mediated by the enzyme Na-K-ATPase, which is found on both pigmented and non-pigmented ciliary epithelia. Na-K-ATPase is of specific pharmacological interest as many different molecules, including cardiac glycosides, dinitrophenol, and acetazolamide can inhibit it. Carbonic anhydrase, an enzyme also found in pigmented and non-pigmented ciliary epithelia, mediates bicarbonate transport across ciliary epithelium via hydration of CO, forming HCO and protons.

retrieved_docs.title: Antiarrhythmic Medications -- Mechanism of Action -- Class II Antiarrhythmic
retrieved_docs.content: Class IId (Muscarinic M2 receptor activators [eg, pilocarpine, carbachol, methacholine, digoxin]): These drugs activate supraventricular (SA node, atrial, AV node) muscarinic M2 cholinergic receptors, hyperpolarizing the SA node, and shortening action potential duration in atrial and AV nodal tissue. These agents also exhibit inhibitory effects on adenylyl cyclase and cAMP activation, resulting in increased RR and PR intervals, reduced SA node automaticity, and decreased AVN conduction. In addition, digoxin is also a Na/K-ATPase inhibitor. Binding with the sodium pump increases intracellular Na + concentration, driving Ca 2+ influx. This activity will lead to increased contractility of the heart and prolongation of phase 4 and phase 0 of the cardiac action potential, thus slowing down conduction through the AVN. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardioactive Steroid Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Electrolyte Imbalances
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia is an indicator of the severity of CAS toxicity. Both hyper and hypokalemia may potentiate digoxin toxicity. Hyperkalemia itself does not cause death, and its correction does not improve survival. Anti-hyperkalemic measures, including insulin/dextrose administration, require judicious use to avoid hypokalemia. Anti hyperkalemic measures along with digoxin immune fab can lead to hypokalemia as the reactivated Na-K-ATPase pump will draw the potassium into the intracellular compartment.

retrieved_docs.title: Digoxin Immune Fab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Digoxin also increases vagal tone in the sinoatrial (SA) and atrioventricular (AV) nodes. [12] This causes prolonged refractory periods and decreased conduction velocity in specialized conductive cardiac tissue. Digoxin toxicity causes a greater degree of Na + /K + ATPase inhibition, resulting in increased automaticity and inotropy. Additionally, a toxicity-induced increase in vagal tone causes excessive inhibition of the AV node, decreasing cardiac dromotropy.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Muscle Myocyte -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Digoxin/digitalis is a drug that is used to treat patients with severe heart failure. Digoxin has been shown to reduce symptoms and hospitalizations without lowering mortality. Digoxin is an ionotropic agent because it increased cardiac contractility. Its primary mechanism of action is through blocking Na+/K+ ATPase pumps (which pumps Na+ out and K+ in) on the myocyte surface. Blocking this pump leads to an increase in intracellular Na+. Due to increased intracellular Na+ and decreased extracellular Na+ concentration, there is a decrease in the activity of a Na+/Ca2+ exchanger on the cell surface, which usually exchanges Na+ into the cell for Ca2+ out of the cell. Thus, digoxin ultimately results in increased intracellular Ca2+ concentrations and increased contractility. Increased Ca2+ also lengthens Phase 2 of the cardiac myocyte action potential and slow the heart rate. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Ibutilide -- Mechanism of Action -- Phase 4: Resting Phase
retrieved_docs.content: Na+/K+ ATPase dominates phase 4. For every 3 Na+ ions pumped out of the cell, 2 K+ ions are pumped in, resulting in a negative resting membrane potential. A primary active transporter called the calcium ATPase re-sequesters most of the intracellular calcium into the sarcoplasmic reticulum. The regulation of sarcoplasmic calcium ATPase occurs by an intracellular protein called phospholamban. When phospholamban undergoes phosphorylation via protein kinase A (PKA), the calcium ATPase is active and incorporates cytosolic calcium ions into the sarcoplasmic reticulum. More calcium is released into the cytosol during the next action potential, causing increased contractility. When phospholamban is de-phosphorylated, it inhibits the sarcoplasmic calcium ATPase.

retrieved_docs.title: Ischemic Cardiomyopathy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Digoxin does not decrease mortality but is helpful in decreasing symptoms and hospitalizations in patients with congestive heart failure (CHF). Digoxin works by inhibiting the effects of the enzyme sodium/potassium ATPase in the heart muscle, stopping the sodium/calcium exchange, leaving more calcium inside the cell which increases contractility.

retrieved_docs.content: GDC Terminology is a classification system that encompasses the concept of inhibitory proton pumps—agents that suppress gastric acid secretion by blocking the H⁺/K⁺‑ATPase enzyme—and also includes digoxin, a cardiac glycoside used to increase contractility in heart failure and control ventricular rate in atrial fibrillation. By grouping these distinct pharmacologic concepts together, GDC Terminology provides a structured reference for clinicians and researchers to locate information on both gastrointestinal acid‑suppression therapies and cardiac inotropic agents within a unified framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1923
retrieved_docs.content: CARDIAC THERAPY DRUGS is identified as a type of srdeční glykosidy (cardiac glycosides), placing it within the well‑known class of agents that increase cardiac contractility by inhibiting the Na⁺/K⁺‑ATPase pump. At the same time, CARDIAC THERAPY DRUGS serves as a broader category that also encompasses other cardiac preparations classified in the ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical) system, indicating that it includes a range of additional cardiac medications beyond glycosides. This dual relationship highlights CARDIAC THERAPY DRUGS as both a specific glycoside subclass and a comprehensive grouping of various cardiac drugs used in therapeutic practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 131
retrieved_docs.content: Collectively, the knowledge emphasizes that COX‑2 selective NSAIDs, carbonic‑anhydrase inhibitors, thiazide and other low‑ceiling diuretics, loop diuretics, Class III anti‑arrhythmics, β‑blockers, and certain antiparasitic agents share a sulfonamide chemical backbone. Recognizing these structural commonalities, the specific mechanisms of COX‑2 inhibition, carbonic‑anhydrase inhibition, Na⁺‑Cl⁻ symporter blockade, potassium‑channel blockade, and phosphodiesterase inhibition, together with the documented contraindications (sulfonamide allergy, dofetilide–hydrochlorothiazide interaction), is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1718
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmic drugs represent a diverse pharmacologic group used to treat cardiac rhythm disturbances. Among them, cardiac glycosides are classified as antiarrhythmics because they modulate myocardial contractility and conduction. Nizofenone, although primarily known as a neuroprotective compound, also exhibits antiarrhythmic activity and is considered part of the antiarrhythmic category. Prokainamide hydrochloride is a well‑established class IA antiarrhythmic agent that blocks sodium channels and prolongs the action potential. Together, these agents illustrate the broad spectrum of mechanisms encompassed by antiarrhythmic therapy, highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate agents based on their specific pharmacologic properties for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1268
retrieved_docs.content: Amiloride is a potassium‑sparing diuretic that functions as an inhibitor of epithelial sodium channels, making it a member of the sodium‑channel‑blocking class of drugs. Its primary physiologic effect is increased diuresis, which occurs without the loss of potassium. In the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification, amiloride is grouped with other potassium‑sparing agents, a category that also includes triamterene. Both amiloride and triamterene belong to the broader class of sodium‑channel blockers, highlighting their shared mechanism of action in reducing sodium reabsorption while preserving potassium balance. This interconnected classification underscores the therapeutic role of these agents in conditions requiring diuretic therapy with potassium conservation.

retrieved_docs.content: انقطاع البول (urinary retention), emphasizing the need to avoid these agents when bladder emptying is impaired. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured network of membrane‑transport modulators, channel blockers, and potassium‑sparing diuretics, clarifying their classifications, mechanisms of action, therapeutic effects, and important contraindications for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1915
retrieved_docs.content: Amiloride is classified as a sodium‑channel blocker and exerts its pharmacologic effect by inhibiting the sodium‑channel activity referred to as ISA. By blocking these channels, amiloride reduces sodium reabsorption in the distal nephron, which underlies its use as a potassium‑sparing diuretic in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 216
retrieved_docs.content: Heart failure (فشل قلبي) is positioned within the broader category of myocardial, endocardial, pericardial and valve disorders and is managed primarily with diuretic and vasodilator therapies while avoiding agents that pose cardiac risk. Amiloride, a sodium‑channel blocker belonging to the triamteren subclass of potassium‑sparing diuretics, increases diuresis and is combined with the saluretic loop diuretic bumetanide (bumetanid) in a fixed‑dose oral tablet (amiloride 5 mg / bumetanide 1 mg) that provides potassium‑sparing diuretic action by coupling sodium‑channel blockade with loop‑diuretic‑induced natriuresis. Bumetanide itself is classified as a saluretic within the “Types and Miscellaneous Diuretics” group. Flosequinan, a quinolone vasodilator, produces vasodilation and is used to reduce cardiac preload and afterload in heart‑failure patients. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1447
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac glycosides constitute a pharmacologic class of compounds that act on the heart to increase contractility and are commonly employed in the treatment of heart failure and certain arrhythmias; within this class, strofantiny is identified as a specific cardiac glycoside, representing a distinct member of the broader group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2506
retrieved_docs.content: Dofetilide is classified as a Class III antiarrhythmic agent and exerts its therapeutic effect through an inverse action on the I_SA (potassium) channel, thereby prolonging cardiac repolarization and helping to maintain sinus rhythm. Because of its potent effect on cardiac electrophysiology, dofetilide is contraindicated for use together with hydrochlorothiazide; the combination can predispose patients to electrolyte disturbances that increase the risk of serious arrhythmias such as torsades de pointes. Careful medication selection and monitoring are essential when prescribing dofetilide to avoid adverse interactions with hydrochlorothiazide.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 466
retrieved_docs.content: Antiarrhythmics constitute a diverse group of cardiac drugs that includes both cardiac glycosides and other specific agents. Cardiac glycosides, exemplified by sukralfát, are classified within the antiarrhythmic drug family because of their ability to modify cardiac conduction and rhythm. In addition to cardiac glycosides, the antiarrhythmic category also comprises drugs such as nizofenone, prokainamid, and the salt form prokainamid hydrochlorid, each of which is used to treat abnormal heart rhythms. Together, these substances illustrate the range of pharmacologic options available for managing arrhythmias, highlighting the importance of recognizing both class-wide mechanisms and individual drug characteristics in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 513
retrieved_docs.content: Other cardiac stimulants in ATC and Phosphodiesterase inhibitors, cardiac stimulant are both classified as cardiac stimulants excluding cardiac glycosides. Within the phosphodiesterase‑inhibitor subclass, agents such as dibutyryl cyclic AMP and milrinone are included. By inhibiting phosphodiesterase enzymes, these compounds raise intracellular cyclic AMP levels, producing positive inotropic and vasodilatory effects that support cardiac function without the mechanisms associated with cardiac glycosides. Consequently, they represent a distinct therapeutic group of cardiac stimulants used in specific cardiovascular conditions, particularly when glycoside‑based therapy is unsuitable.

Overall, the therapeutic landscape links cardiovascular agents, cardiac glycosides, anti‑arrhythmic drugs, and inotropic agents through hierarchical classifications and overlapping pharmacologic actions, while emphasizing specific indications (e.g., low cardiac output, shock, arrhythmia, glaucoma) and critical contraindications such as the adenosine–bepridil interaction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are clinical situations in which the use of digoxin is contraindicated, meaning that digoxin should not be administered to patients experiencing these life‑threatening cardiac arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1944
retrieved_docs.content: Sukralfát is classified as a cardiac glycoside, and cardiac glycosides, prokainamide, and prokainamide hydrochloride are all members of the antiarrhythmic drug group. As antiarrhythmic agents, these substances are used to treat abnormal heart rhythms, with sukralfát representing the cardiac‑glycoside subclass and prokainamide (including its hydrochloride salt) representing other antiarrhythmic compounds. Understanding that these diverse agents share the common function of rhythm control helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with cardiac arrhythmias.
